name: Gummi: A Bendable Computer
short_description: A credit-card-sized handheld whose flexible body is physically bent as a continuous input, paired with a flexible display.
long_description: Gummi is an interaction technique and device concept in which a thin, flexible handheld is deformed by bending as a primary input modality, combined with 2D position control on its surface. Built from layers of flexible electronic components and deformation sensors with a bendable display, it explores how physical flexibility of future flexible electronics could become an interface in its own right rather than a defect to engineer away. As an early demonstration of deformation-based and shape-changing interaction, it is a foundational reference for tangible and organic user interfaces in physical AI and interaction design.
ai_note: Gummi is a deformation-sensing tangible input technique with bend and position sensors and a flexible display; no AI or machine learning runs in the system.

images.caption: The Gummi device and its bending interaction: a thin flexible handheld bent by both hands as continuous input.
images.alt_text: A thin, credit-card-sized flexible handheld device held in two hands and bent into a curve, with a small display on its surface.

interactions.title: Bend to zoom, bend harder to commit
interactions.description: Holding the card in two hands, you flex it to zoom or scroll continuously like an analog joystick; push the bend past a threshold and it registers a discrete "click" that locks in your selection.

interactions.title: Steer a cursor from behind the screen
interactions.description: Your thumb drags across a 2D position sensor on the back of the device to move an on-screen cursor, so your finger never covers the small flexible display while you point.

interactions.title: Browse the web with no buttons
interactions.description: With no buttons or switches anywhere on the body, you navigate a graphical web browser by positioning the rear cursor over a link and bending the whole device to follow it.

interactions.title: Pan and zoom a map by hand
interactions.description: Touch the back of the card to recenter the map on a place, then bend the device to zoom in until it switches into a detailed street view.

interactions.title: Learn the whole interface in minutes
interactions.description: In informal evaluations people picked up bending-plus-rear-touch within a few minutes and described it as easy, fun, and natural, with the rear touchpad an especially popular way to drive a tiny screen.

interactions.title: Flip through photos and play a game
interactions.description: Beyond browsing and maps, the prototype was shown handling image viewing, simple 3D gaming, and text entry, all driven by the same bend-and-touch vocabulary.

interactions.title: Squeeze-to-confirm reading device
interactions.description: An e-reader built on the same bend-as-push, bend-past-threshold-as-click mechanic: lightly flex to page forward at a speed set by how far you bend, and a firmer bend bookmarks the spot, with no rim buttons to fumble for in the dark.

interactions.title: Two-handed accessibility pointer
interactions.description: For someone who finds precise on-screen tapping hard, the rear position pad plus gross bending gesture offers a coarse, whole-hand way to point and select that never requires occluding the display.
